serving.description: 1 serving (stew only, without rice or couscous)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of chicken tagine stew (without accompaniment) is approximately 300–350 g, comprising chicken pieces, vegetables, and sauce; when served with rice or couscous the total plate weight is approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken stew, home-prepared (SR Legacy ID 05315) and composite ingredient estimation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact 'chicken tagine' entry exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy chicken stew (ID 05315) as the closest approved-source equivalent for a slow-cooked chicken and vegetable stew. The input record's per_100g values (energy 136 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 6.4 g, carbohydrate 9.1 g) are broadly consistent with USDA SR Legacy chicken stew (energy ~130 kcal, protein ~11 g, fat ~6 g, carbohydrate ~8 g per 100 g) and are retained with medium confidence. Micronutrient values are cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy chicken stew where available.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on mixed chicken and vegetable stew with low-GI vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken tagine exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated based on the composite profile: slow-cooked chicken (GI ~0, protein-dominant), low-GI vegetables (carrots GI ~35, tomatoes GI ~15, onions GI ~10), and moderate carbohydrate content (9.1 g/100 g). The overall dish GI is estimated at approximately 35 (low), consistent with mixed meat-and-vegetable stews in the international GI tables. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (35 × 9.09 × 3.5) / 100 = 11.1 g. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 172.7 mg/100 g; potassium-rich vegetables (carrots, tomatoes, onions) support favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Chicken tagine has a relatively low sodium content at 172.7 mg/100 g; however, patients with hypertension should request preparation without preserved lemons and limit olives, as both are high-sodium ingredients that can substantially raise the dish's total sodium load — use low-sodium chicken broth and rinse preserved lemons thoroughly if used.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~35), moderate carbohydrate 9.1 g/100 g, low sugar 1.8 g/100 g, high protein 10 g/100 g moderates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken tagine has a low estimated glycaemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (9.1 g/100 g), making the stew itself suitable for type 2 diabetes management; however, patients should carefully portion or avoid the couscous or rice accompaniment, which significantly increases the glycaemic load of the meal, and should reduce potato quantity in the stew if blood glucose control is a priority.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat 1.45 g/100 g, trace trans fat 0.04 g/100 g, olive oil base, anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken tagine is a cardiovascular-friendly dish with low saturated fat (1.45 g/100 g), trace trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and an olive oil base providing monounsaturated fats; the anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, garlic) may confer additional cardioprotective benefit, and patients should ensure the dish is prepared without excessive added salt or high-sodium preserved ingredients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (curcumin/turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, garlic); low saturated fat; nightshade vegetables present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken tagine's anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, garlic) and olive oil base are beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; however, patients with solanine sensitivity should monitor their response to nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, paprika, potatoes, eggplant) and consider substituting potatoes with sweet potatoes, while avoiding high-sodium preserved lemons that may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Tagine is a traditional Moroccan and North African slow-cooked stew prepared in a conical earthenware vessel called a tagine, which creates a self-basting environment that keeps the chicken exceptionally tender and moist. The dish features bone-in or boneless chicken pieces braised with aromatic spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, cinnamon, ginger, and paprika, combined with vegetables such as carrots, tomatoes, onions, and potatoes, and often enriched with preserved lemons, olives, and occasionally dried fruits such as apricots or raisins. The flavour profile is deeply savoury with warm spice notes, a subtle citrus brightness from preserved lemon, and a mild sweetness from the vegetables and optional dried fruit. The texture is rich and saucy, with fall-off-the-bone chicken and soft, flavour-saturated vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 136 kcal, 10 g protein, 6.4 g fat (of which 1.45 g is saturated), and 9.1 g carbohydrate with 1.45 g dietary fibre and only 1.8 g sugar. Sodium is moderate at 172.7 mg/100 g from the base recipe, though preserved lemons and olives can significantly increase this. The dish has an estimated low glycaemic index of approximately 35, making it suitable for blood glucose management when served without high-GI accompaniments. The anti-inflammatory spice blend (curcumin from turmeric, gingerols from ginger, cinnamaldehyde from cinnamon) and olive oil base make it a clinically relevant choice for cardiovascular health and inflammatory conditions. Chicken tagine is traditionally served with couscous, flatbread, or rice, and is a centrepiece dish for Moroccan family meals and festive occasions. It is naturally gluten-free and halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified chicken.

confidence_reason: No exact 'chicken tagine' entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy chicken stew (ID 05315) as the closest approved-source equivalent. The input record's macronutrient values are broadly consistent with this reference and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the composite ingredient profile. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken stew (ID 05315) as no exact chicken tagine entry exists in any approved primary source. Input record values are broadly consistent with this reference and are retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stew preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy chicken stew entry or any other approved source consulted as of 2026-04-16. 3. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy chicken stew reference used; estimated ~280 mg/100 g from ingredient composition but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite tagine preparation are not available in the approved sources consulted as of 2026-04-16. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for chicken tagine is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Input record cultural metadata contained errors: primary_cuisine was listed as 'Turkish' and ethnic_tags included 'Kebab' and 'Shawarma' — these are incorrect for a Moroccan dish and have been corrected to Moroccan/North African. country_of_origin was listed as 'Singapore' which is the serving market context, not the dish origin; corrected to Morocco. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.04 g/100 g is retained from input; this is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in animal products and is not a concern at this level. 8. The input record's health_suitability for 'diabetes' condition has been mapped to the required 'type2_diabetes' key with 'Type 2 Diabetes' as the human-readable condition name per Veritas rules. 9. Multilingual local names provided for Moroccan Arabic romanization (Djaj Mqualli) as the dish's origin language; Singaporean-market multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided given the GrabFood Singapore data source context. G6 multilingual compliance: this dish is Moroccan in origin, not Singaporean, so the strict Singaporean multilingual gate does not apply; however, given the Singapore market context of the input data, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names have been provided as a best-practice addition.
